title: An Exercise in Macroprudential Policymaking

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: A Federal Reserve Bank of New York study examined a June 2015 tabletop exercise in which five Federal Reserve Bank presidents considered macroprudential policy tools to address overheating financial markets. Participants found many prudential tools unattractive due to implementation lags and limited scope. The most favored tools were stress testing, margins on repo funding, and supervisory guidance, with some participants also considering monetary policy as a financial stability tool.
key_points: The tabletop exercise was conducted in June 2015 by the Financial Stability Subcommittee of the Conference of Presidents of the Federal Reserve Banks
Five Federal Reserve Bank presidents participated in the exercise
The scenario presented featured overheating financial markets as the primary financial stability risk
Participants considered capital-based tools, liquidity-based tools, credit-based tools, stress testing, supervisory guidance, and moral suasion
Many prudential tools were deemed less attractive due to implementation lags and limited scope of application
Most favored tools were stress testing, margins on repo funding, and supervisory guidance
Some participants opted for monetary policy as a financial stability tool
Study authors are Tobias Adrian, Patrick de Fontnouvelle, Emily Yang, and Andrei Zlate
why_it_matters: This research provides insight into how senior Federal Reserve officials think about deploying macroprudential tools during periods of financial market stress. Understanding which tools regulators find most practical versus those with implementation challenges can inform expectations about potential policy responses to future financial stability concerns.
macro_context: This study captures pre-crisis thinking about macroprudential policy effectiveness. It reflects the policy toolkit discussion among Fed officials before more aggressive macroprudential actions were taken in response to actual market stress. The preference for stress testing, margins on repo funding, and supervisory guidance over capital or liquidity tools indicates a methodological hierarchy in financial stability thinking.
